title: President Trump signed an Executive Order launching an investigation on imported processed critical minerals and their derivative products
bodyHtml:
On April 15, President Trump signed an executive order directing the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) to launch a Section 232 investigation into the national security implications of importing processed critical minerals and their related products. This investigation may lead to the implementation of Section 232 tariffs on these items, replacing the existing “reciprocal” tariffs currently in effect.

The executive order defines critical minerals as those listed in the U.S. Geological Survey’s (USGS) Critical Minerals List under 30 USC 1606, along with uranium. The latest version of this list was issued in February 2022 and includes 50 minerals. The USGS also emphasized that this list must be reviewed and updated at least once every three years, as required by federal law.

Processed critical minerals refer to those that have gone through post-mining operations, such as refining into metals, metal powders, or master alloys. Derivative products encompass all goods that use these processed minerals as components, which include semi-finished products like semiconductor wafers, anodes, and cathodes, as well as final products such as permanent magnets, electric motors, EVs, batteries, smartphones, microprocessors, radar systems, wind turbines, and advanced optical technologies.

A preliminary report from BIS is expected within 90 days, followed by a final report in 180 days. Based on the findings, the president may consider implementing tariffs or other import restrictions, introducing measures to prevent circumvention of these actions, and enacting policies to boost domestic production, processing, and recycling. These actions may also include other safeguards permitted under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act. The White House noted that any new Section 232 tariffs would replace the current reciprocal tariffs applied to affected products.

The critical minerals listed include:

  1. Aluminum, widely used across nearly all industries
  2. Antimony, found in lead-acid batteries and flame retardants
  3. Arsenic, used in semiconductors
  4. Barite, essential in hydrocarbon extraction
  5. Beryllium, employed in aerospace and defense alloys
  6. Bismuth, used in both medical and atomic research
  7. Cerium, found in catalytic converters, ceramics, glass, metallurgy, and polishing materials
  8. Cesium, applied in research and development
  9. Chromium, mainly used in stainless steel and various alloys
  10. Cobalt, key in rechargeable batteries and superalloys
  11. Dysprosium: Utilized in the production of permanent magnets, data storage devices, and lasers.
  12. Erbium: Applied in fiber optics, optical amplifiers, lasers, and coloring agents for glass.
  13. Europium: Used in phosphors and nuclear control rods.
  14. Fluorspar: Essential in the manufacturing of aluminum, cement, steel, gasoline, and fluorine-based chemicals.
  15. Gadolinium: Important for medical imaging, permanent magnets, and steel production.
  16. Gallium: Found in integrated circuits and optical devices like LEDs.
  17. Germanium: Utilized in fiber optics and night vision technologies.
  18. Graphite: Used as a lubricant, in batteries, and in fuel cells.
  19. Hafnium: Used in nuclear control rods, alloys, and high-temperature ceramics.
  20. Holmium: Found in permanent magnets, nuclear control rods, and lasers.
  21. Indium: Primarily used in liquid crystal display (LCD) screens.
  22. Iridium: Serves as a coating for anodes in electrochemical processes and as a chemical catalyst.
  23. Lanthanum: Used in the production of catalysts, ceramics, glass, polishing compounds, metallurgy, and batteries.
  24. Lithium: Vital for rechargeable batteries.
  25. Lutetium: Found in scintillators used for medical imaging, electronics, and some cancer treatments.
  26. Magnesium: Used as an alloy and for reducing metals.
  27. Manganese: Essential in steel production and batteries.
  28. Neodymium: Used in permanent magnets, rubber catalysts, and both medical and industrial lasers.
  29. Nickel: Key in making stainless steel, superalloys, and rechargeable batteries.
  30. Niobium: Primarily used in steel and superalloys.
  31. Palladium: Employed in catalytic converters and as a catalyst agent.
  32. Platinum: Used in catalytic converters.
  33. Praseodymium: Found in permanent magnets, batteries, aerospace alloys, ceramics, and as a coloring agent.
  34. Rhodium: Applied in catalytic converters, electrical components, and as a catalyst.
  35. Rubidium: Used in electronics research and development.
  36. Ruthenium: Serves as a catalyst and is used in electrical contacts and chip resistors in computers.
  37. Samarium: Important for permanent magnets, as an absorber in nuclear reactors, and in cancer treatments.
  38. Scandium: Used in alloys, ceramics, and fuel cells.
  39. Tantalum: Applied in electronic components, mainly capacitors, and superalloys.
  40. Tellurium: Found in solar cells, thermoelectric devices, and as an alloying additive.
  41. Terbium: Used in permanent magnets, fiber optics, lasers, and solid-state devices.
  42. Thulium: Utilized in various metal alloys and lasers.
  43. Tin: Used as a protective coating and in alloys for steel.
  44. Titanium: Applied as a white pigment and in metal alloys.
  45. Tungsten: Primarily used for manufacturing wear-resistant metals.
  46. Vanadium: Mainly used as an alloying agent for iron and steel.
  47. Ytterbium: Used for catalysts, scintillometers, lasers, and metallurgy.
  48. Yttrium: Found in ceramics, catalysts, lasers, metallurgy, and phosphors.
  49. Zinc: Primarily used in metallurgy to create galvanized steel.
  50. Zirconium: Used in high-temperature ceramics and corrosion-resistant alloys.
